title.zh: 卷四十六 萬石衞直周張傳
title.en: Volume 46: The lords of Wan, Wei, Zhi, Zhou and Zhang
title.raw: 卷四十六 萬石衞直周張傳 Volume 46: The lords of Wan, Wei, Zhi, Zhou and Zhang

sentences.zh: 卷四十六 萬石衛直周張傳第十六

translations.literal: Volume 46: Biography 16 — The Lords of Wan, Wei, Zhi, Zhou, and Zhang.
translations.idiomatic: Volume 46: Biography 16 — The Lords of Wan, Wei, Zhi, Zhou, and Zhang.

sentences.zh: 萬石君

translations.literal: The Lord of Wan.
translations.idiomatic: The Lord of Wan.

sentences.zh: 萬石君石奮，其父趙人也。

translations.literal: The Lord of Wan, Shi Fen, was a man of Zhao by his father’s origin.
translations.idiomatic: The Lord of Wan, Shi Fen, traced his father’s line to the state of Zhao.

sentences.zh: 趙亡，徙溫。

translations.literal: When Zhao fell, they moved to Wen.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhao was destroyed, the family relocated to Wen.

sentences.zh: 高祖東擊項籍，過河內，時奮年十五，為小吏，侍高祖。

translations.literal: When the Supreme Ancestor marched east to attack Xiang Yu and passed through Henei, Fen was fifteen, a petty clerk, and attended the Supreme Ancestor.
translations.idiomatic: While the Supreme Ancestor was campaigning east against Xiang Yu and passed through Henei, Fen—then fifteen—held a minor clerkship and waited upon him.

sentences.zh: 高祖與語，愛其恭敬，問曰：「若何有？」

translations.literal: The Supreme Ancestor spoke with him, loved his deference and respect, and asked, “What do you have?”
translations.idiomatic: The emperor found him respectful in conversation and asked what family he had.

sentences.zh: 對曰；

translations.literal: He replied;
translations.idiomatic: Fen answered:

sentences.zh: 「有母，不幸失明。

translations.literal: “I have a mother who, unhappily, has lost her sight.
translations.idiomatic: “My mother has gone blind, I am sorry to say.

sentences.zh: 家貧。

translations.literal: The family is poor.
translations.idiomatic: We are poor.

sentences.zh: 有姊，能鼓瑟。」

translations.literal: I have an elder sister who can play the se.”
translations.idiomatic: My elder sister plays the se zither well.”

sentences.zh: 高祖曰：「若能從我乎？」

translations.literal: The Supreme Ancestor said, “If you can, will you follow me?”
translations.idiomatic: The Supreme Ancestor said, “Will you come with me?”

sentences.zh: 曰：「願盡力。」

translations.literal: He said, “I wish to exert my utmost strength.”
translations.idiomatic: “I will serve you with all I have,” he said.

sentences.zh: 於是高祖召其姊為美人，以奮為中涓，受書謁。

translations.literal: Thereupon the Supreme Ancestor summoned his sister to be a Meiren and appointed Fen as palace usher (zhongjuan) to receive petitions and calling cards.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor then took Fen’s sister into the harem as a Meiren beauty and made Fen a zhongjuan usher to handle petitions and callers.

sentences.zh: 徙其家長安中戚里，以姊為美人故也。

translations.literal: He moved the family to Qili in Chang’an because of the sister’s rank as Meiren.
translations.idiomatic: Their household was settled in Qili ward in Chang’an on account of her appointment.

sentences.zh: 奮積功勞，孝文時官至太中大夫。

translations.literal: Fen accumulated merit through service; under Emperor Wen he rose to Grand Counselor of the Palace.
translations.idiomatic: By steady service under Emperor Wen he reached the rank of Grand Counselor of the Palace.

sentences.zh: 無文學，恭謹，舉無與比。

translations.literal: He had no literary learning, but was respectful and cautious; none could be compared with him in that.
translations.idiomatic: He was no scholar, yet in courtesy and care no one was his match.

sentences.zh: 東陽侯張相如為太子太傅，免。

translations.literal: Marquis of Dongyang Zhang Xiangru was Grand Tutor to the Heir Apparent and was dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: When Marquis of Dongyang Zhang Xiangru vacated the post of Grand Tutor to the heir apparent,

sentences.zh: 選可為傅者，皆推奮太子太傅。

translations.literal: In choosing who could serve as tutor, all recommended Fen as Grand Tutor to the Heir Apparent.
translations.idiomatic: the court unanimously nominated Fen for Grand Tutor.

sentences.zh: 及孝景即位，以奮為九卿。

translations.literal: When Emperor Jing ascended the throne, he made Fen one of the Nine Ministers.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Jing raised him to one of the Nine Ministers.

sentences.zh: 迫近，憚之，徙奮為諸侯相。

translations.literal: Because he was close at hand and they feared him, they transferred Fen to be chancellor to the feudal lords.
translations.idiomatic: Finding him too near and formidable, they moved him to a chancellorship among the feudal states.

sentences.zh: 奮長子建，次甲，次乙，次慶，皆以馴行孝謹，官至二千石。

translations.literal: Fen’s eldest son was Jian, next Jia, next Yi, next Qing; all by docile conduct, filial piety, and caution reached salaries of two thousand piculs.
translations.idiomatic: His sons Jian, Jia, Yi, and Qing—every one dutiful, filial, and careful—each rose to two-thousand-picul office.

sentences.zh: 於是景帝曰：「石君及四子皆二千石，人臣尊寵乃舉集其門。」

translations.literal: Thereupon Emperor Jing said, “Lord Shi and his four sons all hold two-thousand-picul posts; a subject’s honor and favor have gathered in a single gate.”
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Jing remarked, “The Shi household now holds five posts at two thousand piculs; favor has massed at one door.”

sentences.zh: 凡號奮為萬石君。

translations.literal: In all they called Fen the Lord of Wan.
translations.idiomatic: Hence people called him the Lord of Wan—the “ten thousand piculs” lord.

sentences.zh: 孝景季年，萬石君以上大夫祿歸老于家，以歲時為朝臣。

translations.literal: In the last years of Emperor Jing, the Lord of Wan retired to his home on the salary of a senior grandee and, by the seasons, attended court as a minister.
translations.idiomatic: In Emperor Jing’s closing years he retired on a senior grandee’s stipend, appearing at court only on seasonal occasions.

sentences.zh: 過宮門闕必下車趨，見路馬必軾焉。

translations.literal: Passing palace gates and towers he always dismounted and hurried on foot; seeing the ruler’s horses on the road he always laid his hand on the chariot rail in salute.
translations.idiomatic: He would dismount and quicken his step at every palace gate; if he saw the emperor’s horses on the road he touched the chariot rail in respect.

sentences.zh: 子孫謂小吏，來歸謁，萬石君必朝服見之，不名。

translations.literal: When grandsons who were petty clerks came home to pay their respects, the Lord of Wan always received them in court dress and never called them by personal name.
translations.idiomatic: When a grandson who held even a minor post came to visit, he received him in full court dress and never used a familiar name.

sentences.zh: 子孫有過失，不誚讓，側，雖燕必冠，申申如也。

translations.literal: If grandsons had faults he did not scold aloud; he turned aside; even at ease he always wore his cap, and was grave and composed.
translations.idiomatic: When they erred he offered no sharp rebuke—only a turned shoulder—yet even in private he kept his cap straight and his bearing solemn.

sentences.zh: 僮僕訢訢如也，唯謹。

translations.literal: The household servants were cheerful and mild; all was done with utmost care.
translations.idiomatic: The servants were easy and orderly; discipline ran through every detail.

sentences.zh: 上時賜食於家，必稽首俯伏而食，如在上前。

translations.literal: When the emperor sent food to his house, he always knocked his head on the ground and ate prostrate, as if the ruler stood before him.
translations.idiomatic: Imperial gifts of food he accepted on his knees, touching his forehead to the floor, as though dining in the ruler’s presence.

sentences.zh: 其執喪，哀戚甚。

translations.literal: In conducting mourning he was deeply grief-stricken.
translations.idiomatic: In mourning he showed profound grief.

sentences.zh: 子孫遵教，亦如之。

translations.literal: The grandsons followed his teaching and did likewise.
translations.idiomatic: The younger generations imitated him in this as in everything else.

sentences.zh: 萬石君家以孝謹聞乎郡國，雖齊魯諸儒質行，皆自以為不及也。

translations.literal: The Lord of Wan’s house was known through the commanderies and kingdoms for filial piety and caution; even the Confucian gentlemen of Qi and Lu, for all their plain virtue, thought themselves inferior.
translations.idiomatic: Word of their filial care spread through every province; the sober Confucians of Qi and Lu conceded they could not equal the household.

sentences.zh: 建元二年，郎中令王臧以文學獲罪皇太后。

translations.literal: In the second year of the Jianyuan era, Palace Commandant Wang Zang was condemned for literary learning by the Grand Empress Dowager.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianyuan 2 the Grand Empress Dowager punished Palace Commandant Wang Zang for his scholarly faction.

sentences.zh: 太后以為儒者文多質少，今萬石君家不言而躬行，乃以長子建為郎中令，少子慶為內史。

translations.literal: The Grand Empress Dowager held that Confucians had much ornament and little substance; now the Lord of Wan’s house did not speak but acted; she therefore made the eldest son Jian Palace Commandant and the youngest son Qing Secretary of the Capital.
translations.idiomatic: She distrusted bookish rhetoric and prized the Shis’ silent example, so she named Jian Palace Commandant and Qing Secretary of the Capital.

sentences.zh: 建老白首，萬石君尚無恙。

translations.literal: Jian was old and white-haired, yet the Lord of Wan was still hale.
translations.idiomatic: Jian’s hair had turned white while his father remained vigorous.

sentences.zh: 每五日洗沐歸謁親，入子舍，竊問侍者，取親中茕廁牏，身自澣洒，復與侍者，不敢令萬石君知之，以為常。

translations.literal: Every five days, on bath-leave, he would return to visit his parents, enter their quarters, secretly ask the attendants, take his father’s privy pail liner, wash and rinse it himself, return it to the attendants, and never let the Lord of Wan know—making this his constant practice.
translations.idiomatic: On each five-day rest he went home, slipped into his parents’ rooms, took the old man’s bedpan screen from the servants, scrubbed it himself, and handed it back—all without Father’s knowledge, week after week.

sentences.zh: 建奏事於上前，即有可言，屏人乃言極切；

translations.literal: When Jian reported affairs before the emperor, if there was something to be said he sent others away and spoke with utmost frankness;
translations.idiomatic: Before the throne he spoke his mind bluntly once the room was cleared;

sentences.zh: 至廷見，如不能言者。

translations.literal: at court audience he seemed unable to speak.
translations.idiomatic: in open session he looked tongue-tied.

sentences.zh: 上以是親而禮之。

translations.literal: For this the emperor drew close to him and treated him with ceremony.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor therefore trusted and honored him.

sentences.zh: 萬石君徙居陵里。

translations.literal: The Lord of Wan moved his residence to Mausoleum ward.
translations.idiomatic: The household later moved to Mausoleum ward.

sentences.zh: 內史慶醉歸，入外門不下車。

translations.literal: Secretary of the Capital Qing, drunk, came home and did not get out of his chariot at the outer gate.
translations.idiomatic: Qing, drunk one night, rode through the outer gate without dismounting.

sentences.zh: 萬石君聞之，不食。

translations.literal: When the Lord of Wan heard of it, he refused to eat.
translations.idiomatic: The old man heard and went on a hunger strike.

sentences.zh: 慶恐，肉袒謝請罪，不許。

translations.literal: Qing was afraid; he bared his shoulder and begged forgiveness; it was not granted.
translations.idiomatic: Qing stripped to the waist to apologize; the father would not relent.

sentences.zh: 舉宗及兄建肉袒，萬石君讓曰：「內史貴人，入閭里，里中長老皆走匿，而內史坐車中自如，固當！」

translations.literal: The whole clan and his elder brother Jian bared their shoulders; the Lord of Wan rebuked him, saying, “The Secretary of the Capital is a nobleman; when he enters the lane the village elders all flee and hide, yet he sits in his chariot at ease—as indeed he should!”
translations.idiomatic: The whole family, with Jian, bared their shoulders until the patriarch cried, “A capital secretary is a grandee: the elders scatter when you enter the lane, yet you ride through at ease—how fitting!”

sentences.zh: 乃謝罷慶。

translations.literal: Thereupon he excused Qing and dismissed him.
translations.idiomatic: Only then did he forgive Qing and send him off.

sentences.zh: 慶及諸子入里門，趨至家。

translations.literal: Qing and his sons thereafter entered the lane gate at a run and hurried home.
translations.idiomatic: After that Qing and his sons dismounted at the ward gate and ran the rest of the way.

sentences.zh: 萬石君元朔五年卒，建哭泣哀思，杖乃能行。

translations.literal: In the fifth year of Yuanshuo the Lord of Wan died; Jian wept in grief so that he needed a staff to walk.
translations.idiomatic: He died in Yuanshuo 5; Jian mourned until he could barely walk without a staff.

sentences.zh: 歲餘，建亦死。

translations.literal: After a little more than a year Jian also died.
translations.idiomatic: A year later Jian followed him.

sentences.zh: 諸子孫咸孝，然建最甚，甚於萬石君。

translations.literal: All the grandsons were filial, but Jian was the most so—more than even the Lord of Wan.
translations.idiomatic: Every descendant was dutiful, yet none surpassed Jian—not even the old lord himself.

sentences.zh: 建為郎中令，奏事下，建讀之，驚恐曰：「書『馬』者與尾而五，今乃四，不足一，獲譴死矣！」

translations.literal: When Jian was Palace Commandant, a document came down from above; Jian read it and said in alarm, “In writing ‘horse’ one joins the tail and has five strokes; now there are only four—one short; I shall die for the fault!”
translations.idiomatic: As Palace Commandant he once read an edict and panicked: “The character ma ‘horse’ should have five strokes; this copy has four—I am a dead man for the blunder!”

sentences.zh: 其為謹慎，雖他皆如是。

translations.literal: Such was his caution; in other matters it was always thus.
translations.idiomatic: That was his level of care in everything he did.

sentences.zh: 慶為太僕，御出，上問車中幾馬，慶以策數馬畢，舉手曰：「六馬。」

translations.literal: When Qing was Grand Coachman and drove the emperor out, the emperor asked how many horses were in the team; Qing tapped each horse with his whip, finished counting, raised his hand, and said, “Six horses.”
translations.idiomatic: As Grand Coachman he drove the imperial equipage; when asked how many horses drew it, he counted each with his crop and answered, “Six.”

sentences.zh: 慶於兄弟最為簡易矣，然猶如此。

translations.literal: Among the brothers Qing was the most easygoing, yet even he was like this.
translations.idiomatic: Qing was the least rigid of the brothers—yet even he was that meticulous.

sentences.zh: 出為齊相，齊國慕其家行，不治而齊國大治，為立石相祠。

translations.literal: He went out as chancellor of Qi; the kingdom admired his family’s conduct; without harsh rule Qi was greatly ordered, and they erected a stone shrine to the chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: Sent to Qi as chancellor, he governed lightly; the state admired the family’s reputation, order followed without severity, and the people set up a stone shrine in his honor.

sentences.zh: 元狩元年，上立太子，選群臣可傅者，慶自沛守為太子太傅，七歲遷御史大夫。

translations.literal: In the first year of Yuanshou the emperor established the Heir Apparent and chose from the ministers who could tutor him; Qing went from governor of Pei to Grand Tutor to the Heir Apparent, and after seven years was promoted Grand Clerk.
translations.idiomatic: In Yuanshou 1 the heir apparent was chosen ministers to instruct him; Qing rose from governor of Pei to Grand Tutor, then after seven years to Grand Clerk.

sentences.zh: 元鼎五年，丞相趙周坐酎金免，制詔御史：「萬石君先帝尊之，子孫至孝，其以御史大夫慶為丞相，封牧丘侯。」

translations.literal: In the fifth year of Yuanding Chancellor Zhao Zhou was dismissed for the fault of the sacrificial gold; an edict to the Imperial Clerk said, “The Lord of Wan was honored by the late emperor; his descendants are supremely filial; let Grand Clerk Qing be made chancellor and enfeoffed as Marquis of Muqiu.”
translations.idiomatic: In Yuanding 5 Chancellor Zhao Zhou lost his post over the vassals’ gold offering; an edict named Qing chancellor and Marquis of Muqiu, citing the late emperor’s regard for the Shi line.

sentences.zh: 是時漢方南誅兩越，東擊朝鮮，北逐匈奴，西伐大宛，中國多事。

translations.literal: At that time Han was in the south destroying the two Yue, in the east attacking Joseon, in the north driving off the Xiongnu, and in the west campaigning against Dayuan; the central lands had many affairs.
translations.idiomatic: The empire was at war on every frontier—Yue, Joseon, the Xiongnu, and Dayuan—while the heartland churned with business.

sentences.zh: 天子巡狩海內，修古神祠，封禪，興禮樂。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven toured the realm within the seas, restored ancient shrines, performed the Feng and Shan sacrifices, and promoted ritual and music.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor toured the empire, refurbished ancient cults, carried out Feng and Shan, and revived rites and music.

sentences.zh: 公家用少，桑弘羊等致利，王溫舒之屬峻法，兒寬等推文學，九卿更進用事，事不關決於慶，慶醇謹而已。

translations.literal: Public expense was tight; Sang Hongyang and others brought profit; men like Wang Wenshu enforced harsh law; Ni Kuan and others pushed literary learning; the Nine Ministers rotated in power; affairs did not await Qing’s decision—Qing was only pure and careful.
translations.idiomatic: Treasury was lean: Sang Hongyang squeezed revenue, Wang Wenshu’s faction wielded harsh codes, Ni Kuan’s circle pushed scholarship; ministers jostled for influence while Qing, ever cautious, scarcely decided a thing.

sentences.zh: 在位九歲，無能有所匡言。

translations.literal: In office nine years he was able to offer no corrective counsel.
translations.idiomatic: Nine years in the chancellorship passed without a memorable policy from him.

sentences.zh: 嘗欲請治上近臣所忠、九卿咸宣，不能服，反受其過，贖罪。

translations.literal: He once wished to impeach the emperor’s close attendant Suozhong and Minister Xian Xuan but could not prevail and instead suffered blame, paying a fine in lieu.
translations.idiomatic: He tried to indict the favorites Suozhong and Xian Xuan, failed, and was fined for his trouble.

sentences.zh: 元封四年，關東流民二百萬口，無名數者四十萬，公卿議欲請徙流民於邊以適之。

translations.literal: In the fourth year of Yuanfeng two million displaced people were in the east of the passes, and four hundred thousand lacked household registration; the dukes and ministers debated asking to move the displaced to the frontier to punish them.
translations.idiomatic: In Yuanfeng 4 some two million refugees thronged east of the passes, four hundred thousand unregistered; ministers proposed shipping them to the border as punishment.

sentences.zh: 上以為慶老謹，不能與其議，乃賜丞相告歸，而案御史大夫以下議為請者。

translations.literal: The emperor thought Qing old and cautious and could not join in that debate, so he gave the chancellor leave to go home while he investigated the Grand Clerk downward for those who had favored the proposal.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor, deeming Qing too timid for such a fight, sent him on sick leave and turned on the officials who had backed the plan.

sentences.zh: 慶慚不任職，上書曰：「臣幸得待罪丞相，疲駑無以輔治。

translations.literal: Qing was ashamed at not fulfilling his duties and submitted a memorial saying, “I have been fortunate enough to hold the post of chancellor; I am a worn-out nag unable to assist in governing.
translations.idiomatic: Mortified, he wrote, “I have disgraced the chancellorship; like a spent horse I can no longer help govern.

sentences.zh: 城郭倉廩空虛，民多流亡，罪當伏斧質，上不忍致法。

translations.literal: City walls and granaries are empty; the people mostly wander in exile; my crime deserves the axe and block, yet Your Majesty will not apply the law to the full.
translations.idiomatic: Our storehouses are bare and the people scattered; I deserve execution, yet you show mercy.

sentences.zh: 願歸丞相侯印，乞骸骨歸，避賢者路。」

translations.literal: I wish to return the chancellor’s seal and ribbon of the marquis, beg leave for my bones to go home, and clear the way for worthier men.”
translations.idiomatic: I beg to surrender seal and ribbon and retire, making room for abler men.”

sentences.zh: 上報曰：「間者，河水滔陸，泛濫十餘郡，隄防勤勞，弗能讯塞，朕甚憂之。

translations.literal: The emperor replied, saying, “Recently the Yellow River overflowed the dry land, flooded more than ten commanderies, and though dikes and levees toiled, they could not be secured; I am deeply troubled.
translations.idiomatic: The reply ran: “Lately the river has overrun its banks and swamped a dozen commanderies; dikes have failed despite every effort, and I am gravely worried.

sentences.zh: 是故巡方州，禮嵩嶽，通八神，以合宣房。

translations.literal: Therefore I have toured the provinces, sacrificed at Mount Song, communicated with the Eight Gods, to join with Xuanyuan House.
translations.idiomatic: So I have inspected the regions, worshipped at Mount Song, and linked the Eight Spirits with the Xuanyuan rite.

sentences.zh: 濟淮江，歷山濱海，問百年民所疾苦。

translations.literal: I crossed the Ji, Huai, and Yangzi, passed mountains and skirted the sea, and asked centenarians what pained the people.
translations.idiomatic: I crossed the Ji, Huai, and Chang Jiang, traced coast and hill, and asked the aged what ailed the common folk.

sentences.zh: 惟吏多私，徵求無已，去者便，居者擾，故為流民法，以禁重賦。

translations.literal: Only the clerks are much given to private ends; exactions never cease; those who leave find it easy, those who stay are harassed; therefore I made a law for displaced persons to forbid heavy levies.
translations.idiomatic: Local officials still line their pockets and tax without end, driving people from their homes; I issued statutes for refugees to curb extortion.

sentences.zh: 乃者封泰山，皇天嘉況，神物並見。

translations.literal: Recently I enfeoffed Mount Tai; High Heaven showed favor; divine tokens appeared together.
translations.idiomatic: At the recent Feng on Mount Tai Heaven signaled approval and omens multiplied.

sentences.zh: 朕方答氣應，未能承意，是以切比閭里，知吏姦邪。

translations.literal: I am answering the qi and responding to portents and have not yet grasped your meaning; therefore I have gone close to the villages to learn clerical corruption.
translations.idiomatic: I meant to answer Heaven’s signs, yet I still probe village by village for official malfeasance.

sentences.zh: 委任有司，然則官曠民愁，盜賊公行。

translations.literal: I entrusted matters to the responsible offices; yet offices stood idle and the people grew bitter, and bandits walked openly.
translations.idiomatic: I left affairs to the bureaus—only to see posts neglected, popular resentment rise, and robbery flourish in the open.

sentences.zh: 往年覲明堂，赦殊死，無禁錮，咸自新，與更始。

translations.literal: In former years I visited the Bright Hall, pardoned capital crimes, lifted prohibitions and bonds, so all might renew themselves with a fresh start.
translations.idiomatic: Last time I visited the Bright Hall I spared capital offenders and lifted legal disabilities so men could begin anew.

sentences.zh: 今流民愈多，計文不改，君不繩責長吏，而請以興徙四十萬口，搖蕩百姓，孤兒幼年未滿十歲，無罪而坐率，朕失望焉。

translations.literal: Now the displaced grow more numerous; the accounts are unchanged; you do not bind and punish the chief officials, yet you ask to move four hundred thousand mouths, shaking the people; orphans not yet ten, without crime, would sit under collective guilt—I am disappointed.
translations.idiomatic: Refugees increase while your tallies lie; instead of punishing magistrates you would uproot four hundred thousand innocents, even children under ten, by mass banishment—I expected better.

sentences.zh: 今君上書言倉庫城郭不充實，民多貧，盜賊眾，請入粟為庶人。

translations.literal: Now you submit a memorial saying granaries and walls are not full, the people are many and poor, bandits are numerous, and you ask to yield grain and become a commoner.
translations.idiomatic: Now you write that granaries gape, the poor swarm, thieves multiply, and you offer to pay grain and resign.

sentences.zh: 夫懷知民貧而請益賦，動危之而辭位，欲安歸難乎？

translations.literal: You harbor the knowledge that the people are poor yet ask to increase levies; you stir danger yet leave your post—where do you think difficulty will go?”
translations.idiomatic: You know their want yet talk of new taxes; you stir crisis then quit—how is that to bring peace?”

sentences.zh: 君其反室！」

translations.literal: You shall return to your quarters!”
translations.idiomatic: Go back to your office!”

sentences.zh: 慶素質，見詔報反室，自以為得許，欲上印綬。

translations.literal: Qing was by nature plain; seeing the edict say “return to your quarters,” he thought he had been permitted and wished to hand up his seal and ribbon.
translations.idiomatic: Plain-spoken Qing read “return to your quarters” as permission to resign and reached for his seal.

sentences.zh: 掾史以為見責甚深，而終以反室者，醜惡之辭也。

translations.literal: The clerks thought the blame was very deep and that “return to quarters” in the end was ugly language.
translations.idiomatic: His staff read the phrase as vicious mockery, not leave to retire.

sentences.zh: 或勸慶宜引決。

translations.literal: Some urged Qing that he ought to take his own life.
translations.idiomatic: Some told him he should fall on his sword.

sentences.zh: 慶甚懼，不知所出，遂復起視事。

translations.literal: Qing was greatly afraid, knew not what to do, and thereupon rose again to handle affairs.
translations.idiomatic: Terror-stricken and unsure, he went back to his desk the next morning.

sentences.zh: 慶為丞相，文深審謹，無他大略。

translations.literal: As chancellor Qing was deep in caution and care and had no other grand strategy.
translations.idiomatic: As chancellor he was meticulous but offered no larger vision.

sentences.zh: 後三歲餘薨，諡曰恬侯。

translations.literal: After a little more than three years he died; his posthumous title was Marquis Tian (“Quiet”).
translations.idiomatic: He died three years later with the posthumous name Quiet Marquis.

sentences.zh: 中子德，慶愛之。

translations.literal: The middle son De was whom Qing loved.
translations.idiomatic: He doted on his middle son, De.

sentences.zh: 上以德嗣，後為太常，坐法免，國除。

translations.literal: The emperor had De inherit; later he became Grand Master of Ceremonies, was dismissed for violating the law, and the marquisate was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor let De succeed, but De later lost the Grand Master post for a legal offense and the fief lapsed.

sentences.zh: 慶方為丞相時，諸子孫為小吏至二千石者十三人。

translations.literal: When Qing was chancellor, grandsons and descendants who were petty clerks up to two thousand piculs numbered thirteen.
translations.idiomatic: At the height of Qing’s power thirteen younger kin held office from clerk to two thousand piculs.

sentences.zh: 及慶死後，稍以罪去，孝謹衰矣。

translations.literal: After Qing died they gradually left for crimes, and the filial caution declined.
translations.idiomatic: After his death they drifted away under charges, and the old discipline faded.

sentences.zh: 衛綰，代大陵人也，以戲車為郎，事文帝，功次遷中郎將，醇謹無它。

translations.literal: Wei Wan was a man of Daling in Dai; by acrobatic chariotry he became a gentleman-at-court and served Emperor Wen, advancing by merit to General of the Household and was pure and careful beyond ordinary.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Wan of Daling in Dai entered the guard corps as a stunt charioteer, rose under Emperor Wen to General of the Household, and was stolidly reliable.

sentences.zh: 孝景為太子時，召上左右飲，而綰稱病不行。

translations.literal: When Emperor Jing was Heir Apparent he summoned those about the emperor to drink, but Wan pleaded illness and did not go.
translations.idiomatic: When still heir apparent Jing had invited the emperor’s attendants to a banquet; Wan declined on grounds of illness.

sentences.zh: 文帝且崩時，屬孝景曰：「綰長者，善遇之。」

translations.literal: When Emperor Wen was about to die he charged Emperor Jing, saying, “Wan is a man of mature years; treat him well.”
translations.idiomatic: Wen’s deathbed words to Jing were, “Wan is a steady man—keep him close.”

sentences.zh: 及景帝立，歲餘，不孰何綰，綰日以謹力。

translations.literal: When Emperor Jing had been on the throne a little more than a year he had not scrutinized Wan, and Wan daily grew more careful and diligent.
translations.idiomatic: Jing never investigated him; year by year Wan only grew more careful.

sentences.zh: 景帝幸上林，詔中郎將參乘，還而問曰：「君知所以得驂乘乎？」

translations.literal: When Emperor Jing favored Shanglin Park he ordered the General of the Household to ride as guard in the chariot; on returning he asked, “Do you know why you were allowed to ride as guard?”
translations.idiomatic: On a visit to Shanglin the emperor made him ride escort and afterward asked whether he knew why.

sentences.zh: 綰曰：「臣代戲車士，幸得功次遷，待罪中郎將，不知也。」

translations.literal: Wan said, “I am a stunt charioteer from Dai who by fortune of merit rose in rank; I await punishment as General of the Household—I do not know.”
translations.idiomatic: Wan said, “I was a Dai chariot acrobat who rose by seniority; I am honored to hold this post—I cannot guess.”

sentences.zh: 上問曰：「吾為太子時召君，君不肯來，何也？」

translations.literal: The emperor asked, “When I was Heir Apparent I summoned you and you would not come—why?”
translations.idiomatic: The emperor pressed: “As heir I called you to a feast and you stayed away—why?”

sentences.zh: 對曰：「死罪，病。」

translations.literal: He replied, “Death-worthy crime—I was ill.”
translations.idiomatic: “I deserve death for it—I was ill,” he answered.

sentences.zh: 上賜之劍，綰曰：「先帝賜臣劍凡六，不敢奉詔。」

translations.literal: The emperor bestowed a sword; Wan said, “The late emperor gave me swords, six in all; I dare not accept the decree.”
translations.idiomatic: Offered a new sword, he said, “The late emperor gave me six; I cannot take another.”

sentences.zh: 上曰：「劍，人之所施易，獨至今乎？」

translations.literal: The emperor said, “Swords are what people exchange; do you alone keep them to this day?”
translations.idiomatic: “Swords are traded every day—have you hoarded yours alone?”

sentences.zh: 綰曰：「具在。」

translations.literal: Wan said, “They are all there.”
translations.idiomatic: “They are all here,” said Wan.

sentences.zh: 上使取六劍，劍常盛，未嘗服也。

translations.literal: The emperor sent for the six swords; they were always full and bright; he had never worn them.
translations.idiomatic: Messengers brought the six blades—still bright in their sheaths, never worn.

sentences.zh: 郎官有譴，常蒙其罪，不與它將爭；

translations.literal: When gentlemen-of-the-palace were censured, he often shielded them and bore the blame himself, not contending with other commanders;
translations.idiomatic: When a subordinate was reprimanded he shouldered the fault himself and never jostled fellow officers for credit;

sentences.zh: 有功，常讓它將。

translations.literal: when there was merit he always yielded to other commanders.
translations.idiomatic: when praise was due he stepped aside and let others take it.

sentences.zh: 上以為廉，忠實無它腸，乃拜綰為河間王太傅。

translations.literal: The emperor thought him incorrupt, loyal, solid, and without other designs, and therefore appointed Wan Grand Tutor to the Prince of Hejian.
translations.idiomatic: The throne judged him honest, steadfast, and free of second motives, and named him Grand Tutor to the king of Hejian.

sentences.zh: 吳楚反，詔綰為將，將河間兵擊吳楚有功，拜為中尉。

translations.literal: When Wu and Chu rebelled, an edict made Wan a general; he led Hejian troops against Wu and Chu with merit and was appointed Commandant of the Capital.
translations.idiomatic: In the revolt of Wu and Chu he commanded Hejian’s host, distinguished himself, and was raised to Commandant of the Capital.

sentences.zh: 三歲，以軍功封綰為建陵侯。

translations.literal: After three years, for military merit Wan was enfeoffed as Marquis of Jianling.
translations.idiomatic: Three years later battlefield service won him the marquisate of Jianling.

sentences.zh: 明年，上廢太子，誅栗卿之屬。

translations.literal: The next year the emperor deposed the Heir Apparent and executed Li Qing and his kind.
translations.idiomatic: The following year the crown prince was cast aside and Li Qing’s faction was put to the sword.

sentences.zh: 上以綰為長者，不忍，乃賜綰告歸，而使郅都治捕栗氏。

translations.literal: The emperor, holding Wan a man of mature years, could not bear to use him in it, therefore gave Wan leave to go home while he sent Zhi Du to arrest the Li clan.
translations.idiomatic: Jing still treated Wan as an elder he would not soil with that purge, sent him on leave, and handed the Li roundup to Zhi Du.

sentences.zh: 既已，上立膠東王為太子，召綰拜為太子太傅，遷為御史大夫。

translations.literal: When that was done, the emperor established the Prince of Jiaodong as Heir Apparent, summoned Wan, appointed him Grand Tutor to the Heir Apparent, and promoted him to Imperial Clerk.
translations.idiomatic: Once the heir from Jiaodong was chosen, Wan was recalled as his tutor and soon advanced to Imperial Clerk.

sentences.zh: 五歲，代桃侯舍為丞相，朝奏事如職所奏。

translations.literal: After five years he replaced Marquis of Tao She as chancellor, and at court he reported affairs as his office required.
translations.idiomatic: Five years on he succeeded Marquis Liu She of Tao as chancellor, filing routine memorials without flourish.

sentences.zh: 然自初宦以至相，終無可言。

translations.literal: Yet from his first office to the chancellorship there was in the end nothing memorable to say.
translations.idiomatic: From first appointment to the highest seat he left almost no policy record.

sentences.zh: 上以為敦厚可相少主，尊寵之，賞賜甚多。

translations.literal: The emperor thought him staunch enough to assist a young ruler, honored and favored him, and bestowed many gifts.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor deemed him steady enough for a boy emperor, showered him with favor, and piled on rewards.

sentences.zh: 為丞相三歲，景帝崩，武帝立。

translations.literal: He was chancellor three years when Emperor Jing died and Emperor Wu ascended.
translations.idiomatic: He held the seal three years; Jing died and Wu took the throne.

sentences.zh: 建元中，丞相以景帝病時諸官囚多坐不辜者，而君不任職，免之。

translations.literal: In the Jianyuan era the chancellor was removed from office on the charge that, while Emperor Jing was ill, many officials and prisoners had been held under unjust conviction, yet you, sir, had not discharged your duties.
translations.idiomatic: Early in Emperor Wu’s Jianyuan reign the court stripped him of the chancellorship, citing the jailings of the innocent during Emperor Jing’s final illness and his own failure to act while in that post.

sentences.zh: 後薨，諡曰哀侯。

translations.literal: Later he died; his posthumous title was Marquis Ai (“Lamentable”).
translations.idiomatic: He died not long after, posthumously styled Marquis Ai.

sentences.zh: 子信嗣，坐酎金，國除。

translations.literal: His son Xin inherited; for the fault of the sacrificial gold the marquisate was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: His heir Xin lost the fief over the vassals’ gold offering.

sentences.zh: 直不疑

translations.literal: Zhi Buyi.
translations.idiomatic: Zhi Buyi.

sentences.zh: 直不疑，南陽人也。

translations.literal: Zhi Buyi was a man of Nanyang.
translations.idiomatic: Zhi Buyi came from Nanyang.

sentences.zh: 為郎，事文帝。

translations.literal: He was a gentleman-at-court and served Emperor Wen.
translations.idiomatic: He served Emperor Wen as a gentleman-of-the-palace.

sentences.zh: 其同舍有告歸，誤持其同舍郎金去。

translations.literal: A man sharing his quarters went on leave home and mistakenly took another roommate’s gold.
translations.idiomatic: A dorm-mate packing for leave accidentally walked off with a neighbor’s hoard of gold.

sentences.zh: 已而同舍郎覺，亡意不疑，不疑謝有之，買金償。

translations.literal: Presently the roommate noticed and suspected Buyi; Buyi apologized as if guilty and bought gold to repay.
translations.idiomatic: When the theft surfaced suspicion fell on Buyi; he accepted blame and bought replacement gold.

sentences.zh: 後告歸者至而歸金，亡金郎大慚，以此稱為長者。

translations.literal: Later the one who had gone on leave returned and gave back the gold; the man who had lost the gold was greatly ashamed; for this Buyi was praised as a man of mature years.
translations.idiomatic: The traveler soon sent the original back; the accuser was mortified, and Buyi’s reputation as a gentleman was sealed.

sentences.zh: 稍遷至中大夫。

translations.literal: He was gradually promoted to Grand Counselor of the Household.
translations.idiomatic: He rose step by step to Grand Counselor of the Household.

sentences.zh: 朝，廷見，人或毀不疑曰：「不疑狀貌甚美，然特毋柰其善盜嫂何也！」

translations.literal: At court, at palace audience, someone slandered Buyi, saying, “Buyi’s looks are very handsome, but what can be done about his habit of seducing his elder brother’s wife!”
translations.idiomatic: At a formal audience a whisperer said, “He is handsome enough—if only he did not carry on with his sister-in-law!”

sentences.zh: 不疑聞，曰：「我乃無兄。」

translations.literal: Buyi heard it and said, “I have no elder brother.”
translations.idiomatic: Buyi only replied, “I have never had an elder brother.”

sentences.zh: 然終不自明也。

translations.literal: Yet in the end he did not vindicate himself further.
translations.idiomatic: He never bothered to argue the point further.

sentences.zh: 不疑學老子言。

translations.literal: Buyi studied the words of Laozi.
translations.idiomatic: His reading ran to Laozi.

sentences.zh: 其所臨，為官如故，唯恐人之知其為吏跡也。

translations.literal: Wherever he served, he acted as an official as before, only fearing that people would know he had been a clerk.
translations.idiomatic: In every post he kept the same low profile, almost hiding the fact that he had ever wielded a brush.

sentences.zh: 不好立名，稱為長者。

translations.literal: He did not love to make a name and was called a man of mature years.
translations.idiomatic: He sought no fame yet was everywhere called a gentleman.

sentences.zh: 薨，諡曰信侯。

translations.literal: He died; his posthumous title was Marquis Xin (“Trustworthy”).
translations.idiomatic: At his death he received the posthumous name Trustworthy Marquis.

sentences.zh: 傳子至孫彭祖，坐酎金，國除。

translations.literal: It passed to a son and on to grandson Pengzu; for the fault of the sacrificial gold the marquisate was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: The title passed to his son and grandson Pengzu until the gold-offering scandal stripped the house of its fief.

sentences.zh: 周仁，其先任城人也。

translations.literal: Zhou Ren’s ancestors were men of Rencheng.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Ren’s family hailed from Rencheng.

sentences.zh: 以毉見。

translations.literal: He gained audience through medicine.
translations.idiomatic: He entered court on the strength of his medical skill.

sentences.zh: 景帝為太子時，為舍人，積功遷至太中大夫。

translations.literal: When Emperor Jing was Heir Apparent, Ren was a household attendant and accumulated merit until Grand Counselor of the Palace.
translations.idiomatic: While Jing was still heir apparent Ren served as household attendant and climbed to Grand Counselor of the Palace.

sentences.zh: 景帝初立，拜仁為郎中令。

translations.literal: When Emperor Jing first ascended the throne, he appointed Ren Palace Commandant.
translations.idiomatic: At the beginning of Jing’s reign Ren became Palace Commandant.

sentences.zh: 仁為人陰重不泄。

translations.literal: Ren’s person was secretive, weighty, and reticent.
translations.idiomatic: Ren was close-mouthed, imperturbable, and impossible to read.

sentences.zh: 常衣弊補衣溺苎，期為不潔清，以是得幸，入臥內。

translations.literal: He constantly wore ragged patched clothes and coarse ramie fouled with urine, intending to seem unclean; by this he won favor and was admitted to the bedchamber.
translations.idiomatic: He affected torn, patched garments and urine-stained ramie so he would look too foul for intrigue—exactly the disguise that won him access to the inner rooms.

sentences.zh: 於後宮祕戲，仁常在旁，終無所言。

translations.literal: During the harem’s private amusements Ren was always at hand yet never spoke.
translations.idiomatic: When the consorts staged their private entertainments he stood by in silence year after year.

sentences.zh: 上時問人，仁曰：「上自察之。」

translations.literal: When the emperor sometimes asked him about people, Ren said, “Let Your Majesty examine them yourself.”
translations.idiomatic: If the emperor asked his opinion of a man, he answered only, “Your Majesty must judge that yourself.”

sentences.zh: 然亦無所毀，如此。

translations.literal: Yet he also slandered no one; it was always thus.
translations.idiomatic: He maligned no one either; his neutrality was absolute.

sentences.zh: 景帝再自幸其家。

translations.literal: Emperor Jing twice in person visited his house.
translations.idiomatic: Jing called at his home twice in person.

sentences.zh: 家徙陽陵。

translations.literal: The household moved to Yangling.
translations.idiomatic: The family later relocated to Yangling.

sentences.zh: 上所賜甚多，然終常讓，不敢受也。

translations.literal: What the emperor bestowed was very much, yet he always declined and did not dare accept.
translations.idiomatic: Imperial largesse flowed freely, yet he habitually refused it.

sentences.zh: 諸侯群臣賂遺，終無所受。

translations.literal: From feudal lords and ministers to common gifts and bribes, he never accepted.
translations.idiomatic: He took nothing—from princes, ministers, or anyone with a purse.

sentences.zh: 武帝立，為先帝臣重之。

translations.literal: When Emperor Wu ascended, as a minister of the late emperor he was held in high regard.
translations.idiomatic: Wu honored him as a survivor of his father’s court.

sentences.zh: 仁乃病免，以二千石祿歸老，子孫咸至大官。

translations.literal: Ren then pleaded illness and retired, returning home on a two-thousand-picul salary; grandsons and descendants all reached high office.
translations.idiomatic: He resigned on grounds of illness, retired on a minister’s stipend, and left descendants who rose to the highest ranks.

sentences.zh: 張敺字叔，高祖功臣安丘侯說少子也。

translations.literal: Zhang Ou, courtesy name Shu, was the youngest son of Marquis of Anqiu Shuo, a meritorious minister of the Supreme Ancestor.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Ou—styled Shu—was the youngest son of Zhang Shuo, the Anqiu marquis who had served the dynastic founder.

sentences.zh: 敺孝文時以治刑名侍太子，然其人長者。

translations.literal: In Emperor Wen’s time Ou, by studying penal names, attended the Heir Apparent, yet the man was one of mature years.
translations.idiomatic: Under Wen he tutored the heir in legalist “forms and names,” though his manner remained that of a gentleman.

sentences.zh: 景帝時尊重，常為九卿。

translations.literal: In Emperor Jing’s time he was honored and often one of the Nine Ministers.
translations.idiomatic: Jing treated him with respect and kept him among the Nine Ministers.

sentences.zh: 至武帝元朔中，代韓安國為御史大夫。

translations.literal: Down to Emperor Wu’s Yuanshuo era he replaced Han Anguo as Imperial Clerk.
translations.idiomatic: In Wu’s Yuanshuo years he succeeded Han Anguo as Imperial Clerk.

sentences.zh: 敺為吏，未嘗言按人，剸以誠長者處官。

translations.literal: As a clerk he never spoke of pressing people; he governed purely as a man of mature years in office.
translations.idiomatic: He never boasted of cracking cases; he ran his bureau as a patient elder.

sentences.zh: 官屬以為長者，亦不敢大欺。

translations.literal: His subordinates thought him a man of mature years and also did not greatly deceive him.
translations.idiomatic: Staff who saw him as a soft touch found they could not easily fool him.

sentences.zh: 上具獄事，有可卻，卻之；

translations.literal: When the emperor sent down prison cases, if they could be set aside he set them aside;
translations.idiomatic: Edicts from the throne that could be softened, he softened;

sentences.zh: 不可者，不得已，為涕泣，面而封之。

translations.literal: if they could not, then unavoidably, weeping, he faced the emperor and sealed them.
translations.idiomatic: when the law left no room, he wept before the emperor, then sealed the dossier anyway.

sentences.zh: 其愛人如此。

translations.literal: His loving of people was like this.
translations.idiomatic: Such was his reluctance to harm others.

sentences.zh: 老篤，請免，天子亦寵以上大夫祿，歸老于家。

translations.literal: Old and infirm, he begged to be dismissed; the emperor also favored him with a senior grandee’s salary and let him retire home.
translations.idiomatic: Age and frailty brought a request for release; the court kept him on a senior grandee’s pay at home.

sentences.zh: 家陽陵。

translations.literal: His home was at Yangling.
translations.idiomatic: He ended his days at Yangling.

sentences.zh: 子孫咸至大官。

translations.literal: Grandsons and descendants all reached high office.
translations.idiomatic: His posterity likewise rose to great posts.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：仲尼有言「君子欲訥於言而敏於行」，其萬石君、建陵侯、塞侯、張叔之謂與？

translations.literal: The appraisal says: Confucius said, “The gentleman wishes to be slow in speech yet quick in action”—does this not speak of the Lord of Wan, the Marquis of Jianling, the Marquis of Sai, and Uncle Zhang?
translations.idiomatic: The historian’s verdict quotes Confucius: the true gentleman is slow to speak and swift to act—do we not see that in the Lord of Wan, the lords of Jianling and Sai, and Zhang the elder?

sentences.zh: 是以其教不肅而成，不嚴而治。

translations.literal: Therefore their instruction was accomplished without severity, and order was achieved without harshness.
translations.idiomatic: Their example brought order without thunder, discipline without the rod.

sentences.zh: 至石建之澣衣，周仁為垢汙，君子譏之。

translations.literal: Yet when it comes to Shi Jian rinsing clothes and Zhou Ren’s filthy disguise, gentlemen have mocked them.
translations.idiomatic: Still, later moralists have smiled at Shi Jian scrubbing bedpans and Zhou Ren’s studied squalor.
